The `CloudantDatabaseException` should basically take the original response status code and use it as its own code.  The goal here was not to mask the actual error code.  So my assumption here was that a 409 would be returned by the `raise_for_status` here: https://github.com/porthunt/python-cloudant/blob/f823d1f68e3431ee63f9f6f4cd9d93a0bf3e39e0/src/cloudant/database.py#L351-L353.  That 409 would then be re-raised as a `CloudantDatabaseException` preserving the original HTTP response code and then re-raised again later as a `CloudantClientException` while still preserving the original HTTP response code.  It appears that the 409 assumption was incorrect?  If so then we have a different problem to deal with.

This is fixing a mild layering violation.  This bit of code wants to steal the linked list of pending items, but wants to make sure that the pending list doesn't reference them any more.  Previously this was done by ensuring that the pending_uniq hash got emptied, but since that hash was removed by this diff it can't do that any more.

Really this code should have been calling `w_pending_coll_drain`; that function pops anything from the list (which will be empty in this case, because we just stole it) and then empties the hash.

After this diff, we use the `art` instead of the hash.  There isn't an equivalent function to `w_ht_free_entries` for `art`, instead we have to destroy and re-initialize; we could chose to continue the layering violation here, but it would look uglier :-)

```Python
class Foo(HasTraits):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        self.observe(...)   # call some observe before super.__init__, which requires _trait_values
        super(Foo, self).__init__(**kwargs)
```

Yeah that is the tricky part which is not yet set on stone.

When we choose to maintain the backward compatibility of previous `api-version=1.0` behavior by default, from now on the majority of current customers and new customers (those whom got bitten by issue #54 and/or whom be persuaded by the warning message in this PR), would then have to do `AuthenticationContext(..., api_version=None)` explicitly. And this situation will last for quite a while until the next major version bump on ADAL. So lots of customers will be on the `api_version=None` side. By the time, we will have 2 options moving forward:

1. We may choose to completely remove the `api_version=...` parameter with a major version bump. That would be a HARD breaking change, because all customers who follow our advice this time would then have to change their code again to remove the `api_version=None`.

2. Or we may choose to keep the `api_version=...` parameter in the API signature, we just will not use it anymore, i.e. it will become a NO-OP. That way it will still be a subtle breaking change (and also shipped with a major version bump), but current `AuthenticationContext(..., api_version=None)` customers would not need to change their code.

All these sound more complicated than [simply remove api_version completely and immediately](https://github.com/AzureAD/azure-activedirectory-library-for-python/pull/56/files), but this is the price to pay for the backward compatibility.

```python
def install(
        runner, overwrite=False, hooks=False, hook_type='pre-commit',
        skip_on_missing_conf=False,
):
```

or 

```python
def install(
        runner,
        overwrite=False,
        ...
):
```

The reasoning here is:
- Prevents diff churn for adding / removing arguments
- Prevents merge conflicts (sometimes)
- Prevents arbitrary indentation that's awkward to do in editors

Historically, C-based systems defined timestamps as seconds since the Jan 1 1970 UTC. People needed a way to extend this sub-second timing. The C world added a struct with both seconds and nanoseconds. Java switched to a long in milliseconds. Python opted for floating point seconds.

Since this is Python, I'm using the floating point convention. I don't think there's a universal answer, but float is nice because it's trivial to case to seconds for systems that need that and is easily encoded as a single value, whether in a database or over the network as JSON.

The assumption here is that the internal value is always a Unix timestamp (in float seconds). We either return it verbatim (as a float) or encode it as an iso string.

It would be possible to generalize this to support datetimes internally, but I'd rather set the precedent that internal timestamps are numbers.
